claimText: * The oldest surviving Buddhist texts, discovered in 1931 in Gilgit (Silk Route).
evidenceExcerpt: * The oldest surviving Buddhist texts, discovered in 1931 in Gilgit (Silk Route).
examUse: Could support a statement-based or institution-feature question.

claimText: * Written on birch bark in Gupta and post-Gupta Brahmi scripts (5th-6th centuries CE), they cover Mahayana Buddhism, medicine, and folklore - written in Buddhist hybrid Sanskrit language of the Gupta Brahmi and Post Gupta Brahmi script of that period.
evidenceExcerpt: * Written on birch bark in Gupta and post-Gupta Brahmi scripts (5th-6th centuries CE), they cover Mahayana Buddhism, medicine, and folklore - written in Buddhist hybrid Sanskrit language of the Gupta Brahmi and Post Gupta Brahmi script of that period.
examUse: Could support a statement-based or institution-feature question.

claimText: * These manuscripts include both canonical and non-canonical Buddhist works that throw light on the evolution of Sanskrit, Chinese, Korean, Japanese, Mongolian, Manchu and Tibetan religion-philosophical literature.
evidenceExcerpt: * These manuscripts include both canonical and non-canonical Buddhist works that throw light on the evolution of Sanskrit, Chinese, Korean, Japanese, Mongolian, Manchu and Tibetan religion-philosophical literature.
examUse: Could support a statement-based or institution-feature question.

claimText: * The Gilgit manuscript contain inter alia Sutras (aphorism) from the Buddhist canon, Samadhirajasutra and the Saddharmapundarikasutra (the Lotus Sutra) form part of the corpus that covers a wide range of subjects including religion, ritual, philosophy, iconometry, folk tales, medicine and many other areas of human life and knowledge.
